topics_flat: Number Theory > Diophantine Equations > Techniques: modulo, size analysis, order analysis, inequalities
Algebra > Equations and Inequalities > Linear and quadratic inequalities
prompt:
After division of a positive integer $n$ by $29$, $41$, and $59$ one has three nonzero remainders such that their sum is equal to $n$.
Find all possible values of $n$.
response_text:
Let the remainders upon dividing \(n\) by \(29,41,59\) be \(a,b,c\), respectively. Then

\[
n \equiv a \pmod{29},\qquad n \equiv b \pmod{41},\qquad n \equiv c \pmod{59},
\]

with

\[
1\le a\le 28,\quad 1\le b\le 40,\quad 1\le c\le 58,
\]

and we are given

\[
a+b+c=n.
\]

We want all positive integers \(n\) with this property.

Since \(a=n-29x,\ b=n-41y,\ c=n-59z\) for some integers \(x,y,z\ge 0\), we get

\[
n=(n-29x)+(n-41y)+(n-59z),
\]

so

\[
2n=29x+41y+59z.
\]

Also, because \(a,b,c\) are remainders,

\[
x=\left\lfloor \frac n{29}\right\rfloor,\quad
y=\left\lfloor \frac n{41}\right\rfloor,\quad
z=\left\lfloor \frac n{59}\right\rfloor.
\]

A cleaner way is to use the remainder condition directly:
\[
n=(n\bmod 29)+(n\bmod 41)+(n\bmod 59).
\]
Hence
\[
n\le 28+40+58=126.
\]
So we only need \(n<127\).

Now split into intervals according to the quotients by \(29,41,59\).

## Case 1: \(1\le n<29\)

Then all three remainders equal \(n\), so
\[
n=n+n+n=3n,
\]
impossible for positive \(n\).

## Case 2: \(29\le n<41\)

Remainders are \(n-29,\ n,\ n\), so
\[
n=(n-29)+n+n=3n-29,
\]
thus \(2n=29\), impossible.

## Case 3: \(41\le n<58\)

Remainders are \(n-29,\ n-41,\ n\), so
\[
n=(n-29)+(n-41)+n=3n-70,
\]
hence
\[
2n=70,\quad n=35,
\]
not in this interval.

## Case 4: \(59\le n<82\)

Remainders are \(n-29,\ n-41,\ n-59\), so
\[
n=(n-29)+(n-41)+(n-59)=3n-129,
\]
hence
\[
2n=129,
\]
impossible.

## Case 5: \(82\le n<87\)

Now
\[
n\bmod 29=n-58,\quad n\bmod 41=n-41,\quad n\bmod 59=n-59.
\]
So
\[
n=(n-58)+(n-41)+(n-59)=3n-158,
\]
thus
\[
2n=158,\quad n=79,
\]
not in this interval.

## Case 6: \(87\le n<118\)

Now
\[
n\bmod 29=n-58,\quad n\bmod 41=n-82,\quad n\bmod 59=n-59.
\]
Hence
\[
n=(n-58)+(n-82)+(n-59)=3n-199,
\]
so
\[
2n=199,
\]
impossible.

## Case 7: \(118\le n\le 126\)

Now
\[
n\bmod 29=n-87,\quad n\bmod 41=n-82,\quad n\bmod 59=n-59.
\]
Thus
\[
n=(n-87)+(n-82)+(n-59)=3n-228,
\]
hence
\[
2n=228,\quad n=114,
\]
which is not in this interval.

So there is no solution in any possible interval.

Therefore no positive integer \(n\) satisfies the condition.

Final answer: There are no such positive integers \(n\).
